coredata.dc:title: Influence of the adsorption effect of graphene quantum dot on the photophysical properties and multiple antioxidant processes of gallic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yajie
Han, Feng
Wang, Lei
Tang, Xingzhu
Sun, Chaofan
Wang, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the influence of graphene quantum dot (GQD) adsorption effect on the multiple antioxidant processes and photophysical properties of gallic acid (GA) has been investigated by density functional theory. Simulated absorption spectra of the GA-GQD covered UVA and UVB regions, and its extremely weak fluorescence intensity means that it is more suitable for use as the sunscreen than GA. Moreover, in the S0 state, the sequence of multiple antioxidant processes of GA (GA-GQD) is: e- → H3 + → e- → H1 + (e- → H2 + → e- → H3 +), and the first step of antioxidant processes of GA-GQD is more straightforward to occur than GA according to the specific thermodynamic parameters. Furthermore, the dynamic simulations of GA and GA-GQD reacted with free radical ·OH revealed that their antioxidant processes are spontaneous, verifying their outstanding antioxidant abilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gallic acid
Graphene quantum dot
Multiple antioxidant processes
Density functional theory
Dynamic simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multiple antioxidant processes in the S0 state
Antioxidant abilities in the S1 state

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometric structures of the GA (a), GQD (b), and GA-GQD (c) in S0 state and the overlapping one hundred initial geometric structures of GA-GQD (fixed the GQD) (d).
IGMH and QTAIM figures of GA-GQD in the S0 state (side view (a) and vertical view (b)).

Absorption and fluorescence spectra (a) of GA and GA-GQD. The FMOs (b) of GA and GA-GQD and their distances between hole and electron (c) of GA and GA-GQD. The white and blue isosurfaces stand for hole and electron, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Multiple antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD and corresponding thermodynamic parameters (Kcal/mol).
Bond length variations of GA and GA-GQD with time.
Total Energy variations with time of GA and GA-GQD reacted with ·OH radical.

The electron density (ρb), Laplacian of electron density (∇2ρb), kinetic electron energy density (Gb), potential electron energy density (Vb), total electron energy density (Hb), and (− Gb/ Vb) ratio at the bond critical points (BCPs) of the GA-GQD.
Adsorption energy (Ead), enthalpy changes (ΔHad), Gibbs free energy changes (ΔGad) and entropy changes (ΔSad) for the adsorption process of GA on GQD in gas phase. All the values are in Kcal /mol.

head.ce:title: Influence of the adsorption effect of graphene quantum dot on the photophysical properties and multiple antioxidant processes of gallic acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the influence of graphene quantum dot (GQD) adsorption effect on the multiple antioxidant processes and photophysical properties of gallic acid (GA) has been investigated by density functional theory. Simulated absorption spectra of the GA-GQD covered UVA and UVB regions, and its extremely weak fluorescence intensity means that it is more suitable for use as the sunscreen than GA. Moreover, in the S0 state, the sequence of multiple antioxidant processes of GA (GA-GQD) is: e- → H3 + → e- → H1 + (e- → H2 + → e- → H3 +), and the first step of antioxidant processes of GA-GQD is more straightforward to occur than GA according to the specific thermodynamic parameters. Furthermore, the dynamic simulations of GA and GA-GQD reacted with free radical ·OH revealed that their antioxidant processes are spontaneous, verifying their outstanding antioxidant abilities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, the impact of ultraviolet (UV) rays on human skin has aroused the attention of researchers. They can cause human skin tanning, even skin cancer in severe cases [1–4]. According to the difference in wavelength, UV rays can be classed as UVA (320–400 m), UVB (280–320 nm), and UVC (200–280 nm) [5–9]. UVC almost does not reach the ground because of the obstruction of the ozone layer, so UVA and UVB are the main factors causing skin damage [10–12]. Moreover, The long-term exposure to UV rays can result in the generation of free radicals by the skin, which can destroy collagen and elastin proteins in the skin and make skin wrinkles, brown spots, and sagging, being the leading causes of skin aging [13–15]. Therefore, discovering and designing an effective sunscreen agent with both UV absorption capacity and antioxidant ability is significant.

Gallic acid (GA) exists extensively in plants, such as chinese nutgall, lacquer tree, tea, etc [16–19]. According to previous studies, the pharmacological agents containing GA perform outstandingly in antioxidants, anticancers, antimicrobials, etc [20]. The applications of GA in the bioactive aspect have been investigated, and the excellent antioxidant ability of GA has been confirmed [21]. Moreover, an emulsion with GA has been explored and it possesses preeminent DPPH scavenging activity [22]. All these studies revealed that GA has favorable antioxidant ability and it can be chosen as a candidate for sunscreen agents. Furthermore, recent works indicate that the graphene quantum dot (GQD) can carry drug molecules to the human body due to its adsorption abilities [23,24]. For example, the carboxymethylcellulose encapsulated sodium salicylate-loaded GQD-crosslinked chitosan hybrid bio-nanocomposite beads bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads can be used as a safe carrier for oral drug delivery [25]. The influence of the size of GQD on protein biological function has been studied and improved the application of GQDs-based biomedical devices [26]. In addition, the GQD can effectively improve the drug-loading efficiency of doxorubicin and siRNA [27]. In addition, the GQD is also an excellent free radical scavenger [28]. These phenomena interest us: can the adsorption of GQD for GA improve the antioxidant activity of GA? Moreover, the UV absorption abilities of sunscreen agents are also critical, so electronic spectra of GA and GA-GQD should also be focused.

In this work, the geometric configurations of GA and GA-GQD in S0 and S1 states were optimized by density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) methods, respectively. The interaction between GA and GQD was investigated by independent gradient model based on Hirshfeld partition (IGMH) and Atoms in Molecules parameters. The adsorption processes of GA-GQD in S0 and S1 states were analyzed by adsorption energy (Ead), enthalpy changes (ΔHad), and gibbs free energy changes. Root mean square displacement (RMSD), frontier molecular orbital (FMO), and hole-electron figures were used to analyze spectral variation of GA and GA-GQD. The antioxidant ability of GA after photoexcitation were evaluated by quantum molecular descriptors. Besides, the thermodynamic parameters were calculated to explore multiple antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD, and their dynamic processes with ·OH free radicals were performed to simulate the real environment.

The initial geometric configuration of the GA-GQD complex was ascertained from one hundred representative geometric structures (shown in Fig. 1 (d)) generated by Molclus [29]. After configuration searching, it can be pointed out that the configuration of GA-GQD is reasonable when GA is paralleled to GQD under 298.15 K. Moreover, this work calculated the max absorption peaks of GA under eight functionals and twelve basis sets in ethanol solvent (listed in Table S1 and Table S2) to select the suitable theoretical level. Combining the experimental result and calculation costs, the geometric structures of GA, GQD, and GA-GQD in S0 and S1 states were optimized using DFT [30–32] and TD-DFT [32–34] methods with PBEPBE/6–31 + g(d), respectively [35,36]. Their absorption and fluorescence spectra were simulated using B3LYP functional [37,38]. By the way, the dispersion correction was added in this work to evaluate the interaction force between GA and GQD more accurately. Furthermore, RMSD, IGMH [39], FMOs, hole and electron, and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis data of GA, GQD, and GA-GQD were obtained by Multiwfn 3.8 [40] and visual molecular dynamics (VMD) 1.9.4 [41].

In addition, the thermodynamic parameters under 298.15 K of GA and GA-GQD were calculated using Shermo [42] in light of the following equations [43,44]:
(1)
BDE = H
A r
O
.
+ H
H
.
- H
A r O H
(2)
PA = H
ArO
-
+ H
H
+
- H
A r O H
(3)
ETE = H
A r
O
.
+ H
e
-
- H
ArO
-
(4)
IP = H
(
ArOH
+ .
)
+ H
e
-
- H
A r O H
(5)
PDE = H
A r
O
.
+ H
H
+
- H
ArOH
+ .
The H(Ḣ), H(H+), and H(e-) enthalpies were obtained according to previous literature [45]. Besides, in this work, the geometric structure optimizations and spectral simulations of GA, GQD, and GA-GQD in gas phase were carried out by Gaussian 16 software [46]. The molecular dynamic processes of GA and GA-GQD were simulated by ORCA 5.0 software [47–49]. The time step was set to 1 fs and ran 100 fs under 298.15 K.

Molecular geometric structures generally significantly influence their photophysical properties and antioxidant abilities [50–53]. Hence, the stable geometric structures of GA, GQD, and GA-GQD in S0 and S1 states were obtained and separately denoted in Fig. 1(a)-(c) and Fig. S1, and the critical atoms were numbered. Obviously, after photoexcitation, the H4 atom of GA-GQD is far away from the GQD, but the distances between H1, H2, and H3 atoms and GQD decrease. To understand the reason for its geometric structure variation, the IGMH of GA-GQD in the S0 and S1 states were plotted in Fig. 2 and Fig. S2, and their bond critical point (BCP) and topological path were also presented. It can be seen that the isosurface between GA and GQD is green, indicating that its interaction force is Van der Waals interaction. Moreover, the relevant electron density (ρb), Laplacian of electron density (∇2ρb), kinetic electron energy density (Gb), potential electron energy density (Vb), total electron energy density (Hb), (− Gb/ Vb) ratio and sign(λ2)ρ at the bond critical points (BCPs) of the GA-GQD in the S0 and S1 states were provided in Table 1 . For all BCPs (except the ∇2ρb of F), ∇2ρb > 0, Hb > 0, and − Gb/ Vb > 1, which revealed that the interaction between GA and GQD is noncovalent. Furthermore, comparing the ρb of GA-GQD in the S0 and S1 states, it can be found that the interaction between O2H2, O3H3 and GQD enhanced, and that between O1H1 and GQD essentially unchanged. However, that between O4H4 and GQD weakened. This result is consistent with the geometric structure variation trend of GA-GQD after photoexcitation.

Besides, to explore the adsorption process of GA-GQD, its adsorption energy (Ead), enthalpy changes (ΔHad), and gibbs free energy changes (ΔGad) were calculated according to equation (6)-(9) and reported in Table 2 , in which, the basis set superposition error (BSSE) was used to correct the binding energies.
(6)
E
ad
=
E
GA - G Q D
-
E
GA
-
E
GQD
+
E
BSSE
(7)
Δ
H
ad
=
H
GA - G Q D
-
H
GA
-
H
GQD
+
H
BSSE
(8)
Δ
G
ad
=
G
GA - G Q D
-
G
GA
-
G
GQD
+
G
BSSE
The ΔHad of GA-GQD is negative, indicating that the adsorption process is exothermic. The ΔGad is less than zero, demonstrating that the adsorption process is spontaneous. In addition, it can be found that the Ead of GA-GQD in the S1 state is lower than that in the S0 state, denoting that the GA-GQD is more stable in the S1 state. All these data manifest that GA can effectively adsorb on the surface of GQD.

To explore the influence of GQD on photophysical properties of GA, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of GA and GA-GQD were displayed in Fig. 3 (a), detailed information was shown in Table S3 and Table S4. The max absorption peak of GA is 278.7 nm, which is located in UVC region. After GA is adsorbed on the surface of GQD, the max absorption peak of GA-GQD redshifts to 321.9 nm (UVA region), and its absorption spectra cover UVA and UVB regions. Moreover, the fluorescence peak of GA is 316.7 nm, situating in UVB region. The fluorescence peak of GA-GQD is 444.4 nm, and its intensity is close to zero. These photophysical properties facilitate the protection of human skin, indicating that the addition of GQD is beneficial.

In addition, it can be pointed out that the fluorescence intensities of GA and GA-GQD weaken compared with their absorption intensity. According to previous studies, molecular geometric structure twisting can cause fluorescence intensity to weaken. Hence, the RMSD figures of GA and GA-GQD between their S0 and S1 states were provided and shown in Fig. S3, and corresponding RMSD values are 0.045 and 0.026 Å, respectively, revealing that their geometry variations are slight upon photoexcitation, which means the reason of fluorescence intensity decreasing is not geometric structure twisting. To figure out why fluorescence intensity weakens, the FMOs and distances between the hole and electron of GA and GA-GQD were plotted and shown in Fig. 3. From HOMO to LUMO, they have an apparent electron transfer phenomenon. Moreover, the distances between the hole and electron of GA and GA-GQD are 1.705 and 3.437 Å, respectively, revealing that the adsorption of GQD can affect the electron transfer of GA and cause fluorescence quenching.

The molecular antioxidant abilities can be evaluated by the three prevailing mechanisms: hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), sequential proton loss electron transfer (SPLET), and single electron transfer followed by proton transfer (SET-PT) mechanisms, which can be described by bond dissociation enthalpy (BDE), proton affinity (PA) and electron transfer enthalpy (ETE), ionization potential (IP) and proton dissociation enthalpy (PDE), respectively [54–58]. Moreover, the lower these parameters, the easier the occurrence of these processes [59–62]. Furthermore, according to recent studies, the molecules that possess catechol units are inclined to perform multiple antioxidant processes (i.e., multiple Ḣ/H+/e- loss processes) due to the formation of o-benzoquinone [63,64]. Hence, the multiple antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD should be investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple antioxidant processes in the S0 state
ce:section.ce:para: The multiple antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD were plotted in Fig. 4 and corresponding thermodynamic parameters were marked. The lowest values were underlined. For GA, comparing the BDEs, PAs, and IP for GA, it is evident that the IP is lowest among them, evincing that GA is prone to transfer an electron to form a cation compound in the first step. Secondly, the cation compound transfers a hydrogen proton in the O3H3 site to form the radical. Moreover, the ETE of the radical is 0.751 Kcal/mol, significantly lower than its BDEs and PDEs. Hence, in the third step, the radical is willing to transfer an electron and form a cation compound. Then, according to our prediction, the cation compound should transfer a hydrogen proton in the O2H2 site to form o-benzoquinone. However, the PDE in the O1H1 site is lowest in all sites, this phenomenon interests us. Through investigating the reaction process, it can be pointed out that the hydrogen proton in the O2H2 site will transfer to O3 atom after losing the hydrogen proton in the O1H1 site. Therefore, the sequence of multiple antioxidant processes of GA is: e- → H3 + → e- → H1 +.

For GA-GQD, in the first step, it loses an electron to form a cation compound. Next, the cation compound transfers a hydrogen proton in the O2H2 site to form a radical. Subsequently, the radical loses an electron to form a cation compound given the lower ETE. Finally, the radical transfers a hydrogen proton in the O3H3 site to form the o-benzoquinone compound. In a word, the sequence of multiple antioxidant processes of GA-GQD is: e- → H2 + → e- → H3 +. According to the above analysis, the adsorption of GQD will change the antioxidant sequence of GA. In addition, the first steps of GA and GA-GQD are both electron transfer processes, and the IP of GA-GQD is lower than GA, which means GA-GQD is more likely to react with free radicals, favoring improving the antioxidant abilities of GA.

To study the antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD in the real situation, their dynamic processes reacted with ·OH radical were simulated, and their relevant bond length and total energy variations were shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 , respectively. Evidently, the O-H bond lengths of GA and GA-GQD increase with time, but their HO-H bond lengths gradually decrease and stabilize around 1 Å. Hence, in the beginning, the corresponding hydrogen proton will transfer to the ·OH radical to form H2O, and then the H2O molecule is away from GA and GA-GQD with time. Furthermore, their energy change trends were fitted and labeled in red dot lines. It can be concluded that their energies gradually decrease with time, evincing that their reaction processes are spontaneous, facilitating the application of GA on sunscreen agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant abilities in the S1 state
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition, to evaluate the reactivities of GA and GA-GQD, their energy gap between HOMO and LUMO, electronegativity (χ), chemical potential (μ), global hardness (η), and global electrophilicity index (ω) were calculated in line with equation (10)-(13) and reported in Table S5. The chemical potential of GA-GQD is lower than that of GA, manifesting that the GA-GQD is more stable. The lower (higher) the global hardness and energy gap (global electrophilicity index), the higher the chemical reactivity [65]. Hence, the reactivity of GA-GQD is augmented compared with GA, and the reactivity is enhanced after photoexcitation, which facilitates the improvement of the antioxidant ability of GA.
(9)
χ = -
1 2
E
LUMO
+
E
HOMO
(10)
μ = - χ =
1 2
E
LUMO
+
E
HOMO
(11)
η =
1 2
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO
(12)
ω =
μ
2
2 η

In this work, the photophysical properties and multiple antioxidant processes of GA and GA-GQD were investigated comprehensively to reveal the influence of GQD on GA. Through analyzing IGMH figure of GA-GQD, it can be pointed out that GQD adsorb GA relies on Van der Waals interaction. Furthermore, adsorption energy of GA-GQD indicates that the GA can effectively adsorb on the GQD, providing the foundation for further exploring photophysical properties and antioxidant abilities of GA-GQD. Compared with GA, the absorption spectra of Ga-GQD cover the UVA and UVB region, and its fluorescence intensity is close to zero, which meets the sunscreen requirement. Moreover, the RMSD and hole-electron analyses demonstrate that the electron transfer process is the intrinsic reason for the fluorescence intensity weakening.

Due to GA having catechol units, its multiple antioxidant processes were studied. The sequence of multiple antioxidant processes of GA is: e- → H3 + → e- → H1 +. It can be found that the number of H1 and H3 is discontinuous because of the formation of IHB. After GQD adsorbed the GA, the sequence of multiple antioxidant processes changes to: e- → H2 + → e- → H3 +. In addition, the first step of the antioxidant processes of GA-GQD is easier to occur than GA, which is favorable to improving the antioxidant ability of GA. Moreover, the dynamic simulation processes of GA and GA-GQD are spontaneous, indicating that GA and GA-GQD have outstanding antioxidant abilities. In a word, this work revealed that using GQD to adsorb GA can effectively adjust its photophysical properties and antioxidant ability, providing a novel idea for designing and synthesizing sunscreen agents. It also can be used in cosmetics to improve their antioxidant abilities. In addition, the molecular stabilities and toxicities of GA-GQD can be further discussed.

Yajie Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Feng Han: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Lei Wang: Investigation. Xingzhu Tang: Investigation. Chaofan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Lingling Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141325.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
